items.title: Understanding Ovarian Cancer Incidence and Histology
items.content: Ovarian cancer is a significant cause of cancer deaths among American women, ranking behind lung, breast, colon, and pancreatic cancers. The most common histologic variant of ovarian neoplasms are epithelial tumors, which can be benign (50%), malignant (33%), or borderline (16%). Among the malignant epithelial tumors, serous tumors constitute 60-70%, while mucinous, endometrioid, and clear cell tumors each account for 10-15%. Age influences malignancy risk in adnexal masses, with younger women more likely to have benign tumors.
keyPoints: Ovarian cancer is a leading cause of cancer deaths among American women.
Epithelial tumors are the most common histologic variant (benign 50%, malignant 33%, borderline 16%).
Serous tumors account for 60-70% of epithelial ovarian malignancies.
Mucinous, endometrioid, and clear cell tumors each make up 10-15% of epithelial ovarian malignancies.
Age influences the risk of malignancy in adnexal masses; younger women are more likely to have benign tumors.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Gynecologic Malignancies | INCIDENCE AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Ovarian Tumor Types and Metastatic Potential
items.content: In addition to primary ovarian malignancies, metastatic epithelial tumors can arise from other organs such as the breast, colon, stomach, and pancreas. Bilateral ovarian masses from metastatic mucin-secreting gastrointestinal cancers are known as Krukenberg tumors. Stromal tumors, though less common, may produce ectopic hormones in certain subtypes. Germ cell tumors share similarities with testicular tumors but differ due to their intraperitoneal location.
keyPoints: Metastatic epithelial tumors can originate from breast, colon, gastric, and pancreatic primaries.
Bilateral ovarian masses from metastatic mucin-secreting gastrointestinal cancers are termed Krukenberg tumors.
Stromal tumors may produce ectopic hormones in certain subtypes.
Germ cell tumors resemble testicular tumors but differ due to their intraperitoneal location.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Gynecologic Malignancies | INCIDENCE AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Ovarian Cancer
items.content: Ovarian cancer has a lifetime risk of approximately 1.6% for American women, with most cases being epithelial tumors. The peak incidence occurs in the fifties to sixties, but it can present from the thirties to nineties. Risk factors include nulliparity, obesity, diet, infertility treatments, and talc exposure, while protective factors are oral contraceptives, multiparity, tubal ligation, aspirin use, and breastfeeding.
keyPoints: Lifetime risk for ovarian cancer in American women is 1.6% (1 in 72).
~19,710 cases expected to be diagnosed in the U.S. in 2024 with >13,270 deaths.
Peak incidence in fifties and sixties; presentation can range from thirties to nineties.
Risk factors: nulliparity, obesity, diet, infertility treatments, talc exposure, hormone replacement therapy.
Protective factors: oral contraceptives, multiparity, tubal ligation, aspirin use, breastfeeding.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.title: Genetic and Pathogenetic Characteristics of Ovarian Cancer
items.content: Ovarian cancers are categorized into type 1 and type 2. Type 1 includes low-grade, indolent tumors with mutations in KRAS, BRAF, PTEN, and PIK3CA. Type 2 is more aggressive, characterized by high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer with TP53 mutation (95%) and loss of BRCA1/2 function. These cancers often originate from the fallopian tube.
keyPoints: Type 1 cancers: low-grade, mutations in KRAS, BRAF, PTEN, PIK3CA.
Type 2 cancers: high-grade serous epithelial, TP53 mutation (95%), loss of BRCA1/2 function.
Type 2 cancers often originate from the fallopian tube.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Testing and Prophylactic Strategies for High-Risk Individuals
items.content: Germline BRCA1/2 mutations significantly increase the risk of ovarian cancer. Women with these mutations should consider prophylactic removal of fallopian tubes and ovaries after childbearing, ideally before age 40, to reduce cancer risk. Prophylactic salpingectomy is crucial for ovarian cancer prevention. Lynch syndrome also increases ovarian cancer risk.
keyPoints: Germline BRCA1/2 mutations increase lifetime ovarian cancer risk (39–63% for BRCA1; 16.5–27% for BRCA2).
Prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy recommended after childbearing, ideally before age 40.
Salpingectomy is key in preventing ovarian cancer.
Lynch syndrome (mutations in MSH2, MLH1, MLH6, PMS1, PMS2) also increases risk.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Symptoms of Advanced Ovarian Cancer
items.content: Advanced ovarian cancer often presents with nonspecific gastrointestinal symptoms such as bloating, early satiety, and abdominal pain. These symptoms are frequently dismissed until advanced stages due to their commonality in primary care settings.
keyPoints: Common initial symptoms include bloating, early satiety, pelvic discomfort, changes in urinary or bowel patterns.
Advanced disease presents with nausea, early satiety, indigestion, constipation, and abdominal pain.
Signs of advanced disease include rapid increase in abdominal girth due to ascites.
Radiologic evaluation shows complex adnexal mass with ascites, carcinomatosis, and adenopathy.
Approximately 70% of women present with stage III disease.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 2)

items.title: Screening and Staging of Ovarian Cancer
items.content: Ovarian cancer screening is not recommended outside clinical trials due to low incidence and lack of mortality reduction in large studies. However, genetic testing for susceptibility genes like BRCA1/2 is appropriate.
keyPoints: Stage I tumors are confined to the ovary; stage II to the pelvis; stage III to peritoneal cavity and retroperitoneal nodes.
Stage IV includes parenchymal metastases or abdominal wall/pleural disease.
70% of patients present with stage III disease; remaining 30% distributed among other stages.
Screening studies have not decreased mortality, even in BRCA1/2 families.
Serum CA-125 is elevated but not specific for ovarian cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Surgical Residual in Ovarian Cancer Treatment
items.content: The amount of residual visible cancer at the end of surgery is a strong predictor of outcome. Achieving an R0 resection (no visible residual cancer) significantly improves survival, while leaving >1 cm of disease results in poor prognosis.
keyPoints: R0 resection leads to median survival >60 months.
Optimal resection (no residual disease >1 cm) offers better outcomes than suboptimal debulking (>1 cm residual).
~30% of tumors that appear confined have already disseminated.
Suboptimally debulked patients derive little benefit from surgery.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.title: Role and Timing of Debulking Surgery in Ovarian Cancer
items.content: Debulking surgery aims to remove as much tumor mass as possible. If large deposits are anticipated, neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking is preferred to reduce morbidity and allow timely chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Interval debulking after neoadjuvant chemotherapy achieves similar outcomes with reduced surgical morbidity.
Median survival for R0 resection post-surgery exceeds 60 months; 28–42 months if macroscopic tumor remains or interval debulking is performed.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.title: Chemotherapy and Survival Outcomes in Ovarian Cancer
items.content: Primary chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin is standard, with intraperitoneal administration being technically challenging. PARPi improves survival for patients with DNA repair defects post-chemotherapy.
keyPoints: 70% of advanced-stage tumors show reduction with optimal debulking and platinum-based chemotherapy; 40–50% achieve complete remission.
PARPi like niraparib, olaparib, or rucaparib improve survival when used post-chemotherapy in patients with DNA repair defects.
Five-year survival rates: Stage I (90–95%), Stage II (70–80%), Stage III (25–40%), Stage IV (10–15%).
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.title: Low Malignant Potential Ovarian Tumors: Management and Prognosis
items.content: Low malignant potential tumors, or borderline ovarian tumors, are typically found in younger women aged 30–50 years. They exhibit indolent behavior with a high 10-year survival rate approaching 98%. However, recurrence is common. These tumors often have mutations such as BRAF, KRAS, and deletions of CDKN2A/2B. Management primarily involves surgery, but targeted therapies for the RAS/RAF pathway and hormonal treatments can be beneficial.
keyPoints: Typically found in women aged 30–50 years
10-year survival rate may approach 98%
Common mutations include BRAF or KRAS and CDKN2A/2B deletions
Management is primarily surgical, with potential benefits from targeted therapy for the RAS/RAF pathway and hormonal treatments
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.title: Stromal Ovarian Tumors: Presentation and Management
items.content: Stromal tumors make up about 7% of ovarian neoplasms, with approximately 1800 cases annually in the U.S. They are most common in women aged 50–60 but can occur at any age. These tumors arise from mesenchymal components and often present as unilateral solid masses detected by abdominal mass or pain due to torsion, hemorrhage, or rupture. Hormonal symptoms may include breast tenderness, precocious puberty, menstrual disturbances, or postmenopausal bleeding. Treatment is primarily surgical resection; chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel is reserved for unresectable or recurrent tumors.
keyPoints: ~7% of ovarian neoplasms are stromal tumors
~1800 cases expected annually in the U.S.
Most common in women aged 50–60, but can occur at any age
Present as unilateral solid masses; may cause hormonal symptoms
Treatment is primarily surgical resection; chemotherapy for unresectable or recurrent tumors
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.title: Germ Cell Ovarian Tumors: Characteristics and Treatment
items.content: Germ cell tumors of the ovary include both benign and malignant forms, such as teratomas and dysgerminomas. Benign teratomas are most common in young women and can undergo malignant transformation into squamous cell carcinomas in older women. Malignant germ cell tumors often present with large ovarian masses and may produce hCG or AFP. They have a higher tendency for nodal or hematogenous metastases compared to epithelial ovarian cancers. Treatment typically involves unilateral oophorectomy, lymph node sampling, and chemotherapy with BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) for advanced cases. Dysgerminomas are highly curable.
keyPoints: Include benign teratomas and malignant forms like dysgerminomas
Benign teratomas common in young women; can transform into squamous cell carcinomas in older women
Malignant tumors may produce hCG or AFP
Higher tendency for nodal or hematogenous metastases than epithelial ovarian cancers
Treatment involves unilateral oophorectomy, lymph node sampling, and BEP chemotherapy for advanced cases
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.title: Understanding the Origin and Treatment Parallels between Fallopian Tube and Ovarian Cancer
items.content: Fallopian tube malignancies are primarily of serous histology, similar to type 2 ovarian cancers. They share biological characteristics and treatment protocols with serous ovarian cancer, often presenting as isolated adnexal masses but spreading early within the peritoneal cavity.
keyPoints: Most fallopian tube cancers arise from tubal epithelium.
Histology is typically serous.
Treatment approaches are similar to those for serous ovarian cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.title: Etiology of Cervical Cancer: The Role of HPV
items.content: High-risk strains of human papillomavirus (HPV), particularly types 16 and 18, are the primary initiators of cervical cancer. These viruses infect epithelial cells near the cervix's transformation zone, leading to high-grade dysplasia and potential malignancy through disruption of cell cycle checkpoints.
keyPoints: HPV infection is the main cause of cervical cancer.
HPV16 and 18 are most commonly associated with high-grade dysplasia.
E6 and E7 proteins from HPV inhibit RB and p53, disrupting cell cycle regulation.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.title: Global Impact of Cervical Cancer: Incidence and Mortality Trends
items.content: In 2024, cervical cancer is expected to result in over half a million new cases globally, with more than 300,000 deaths. The highest incidence rates are observed in Central and South America, the Caribbean, and parts of Africa, where mortality rates are also disproportionately high.
keyPoints: Over 500,000 new cervical cancer cases expected worldwide in 2024.
Estimated >300,000 deaths from cervical cancer globally in 2024.
Highest incidence and mortality rates in Central/South America, the Caribbean, and parts of Africa.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.title: Effectiveness of Pap Smear in Reducing Cervical Cancer Incidence
items.content: The Pap smear is a crucial tool for early detection and prevention of cervical cancer. By identifying preinvasive cervical dysplasia, it allows for timely intervention before progression to cancer.
keyPoints: Annual or biannual screening with Pap smears reduces cervical cancer incidence by detecting premalignant disease early.
Pap smear cytology is highly effective in preventing cervical cancer through early detection and surgical treatment of precancerous conditions.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · HPV INFECTION AND PREVENTION

items.title: Role of HPV Vaccines in Primary Prevention
items.content: HPV vaccines, such as Gardasil-9, are pivotal in preventing high-risk HPV infections and subsequent cervical dysplasia. Vaccination is most effective when administered before the onset of sexual activity.
keyPoints: Gardasil-9 protects against HPV types 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, and 58.
Recommended vaccination schedule: three injections at 0, 2, and 6 months for girls and women aged 9 to 45 years.
Vaccination before sexual activity initiation significantly reduces high-risk HPV infection rates and subsequent dysplasia.
Both boys and girls are increasingly vaccinated to reduce the risk of HPV-induced cancers beyond cervical cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · HPV INFECTION AND PREVENTION

items.title: Risk Factors and Pathophysiology of Cervical Cancer
items.content: Cervical cancer risk is significantly influenced by HPV infection, particularly high-risk subtypes. Other factors include early sexual activity, multiple sexual partners, venereal disease history, smoking, and HIV with low CD4+ T-cell counts. Smoking exacerbates dysplasia in HPV-positive individuals.
keyPoints: High-risk HPV subtypes are prevalent risk factors for cervical cancer.
Smoking is a cofactor increasing the risk of dysplasia with HPV infection.
HIV, especially with low CD4+ T-cell counts, increases high-grade dysplasia and reduces latency to invasive disease.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.title: Symptoms and Physical Findings in Cervical Cancer
items.content: Early cervical cancer is often asymptomatic; larger tumors may present with postcoital spotting, intermenstrual bleeding, or menometrorrhagia. Advanced disease can cause pelvic pain, flank pain from hydronephrosis, or deep-venous thrombosis due to ureteral or iliac vessel compression.
keyPoints: Early cervical cancer is asymptomatic; symptoms arise with larger tumors.
Postcoital spotting and intermenstrual bleeding are common in invasive carcinoma.
Pelvic pain and flank pain indicate advanced-stage disease.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.title: Staging and Treatment of Cervical Cancer
items.content: Cervical cancer staging is crucial for treatment planning. Stage I tumors are confined to the cervix, while stage IV involves distant spread. MRI is preferred over CT for assessing uterine extension. Advanced-stage disease (stage II-IV) often requires radiation therapy with cisplatin-based immunochemotherapy and PD-1 blockers.
keyPoints: Stage I: Confined to the cervix; Stage IV: Distant spread.
MRI is more accurate than CT for assessing uterine extension.
Advanced-stage disease treated with radiation, cisplatin-based immunochemotherapy, and PD-1 blockers.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.title: Risk Factors for Endometrioid Uterine Cancer
items.content: Endometrial cancer, particularly the endometrioid subtype, is influenced by hormonal exposure and genetic factors. Estrogen plays a significant role in early tumor development by promoting gland proliferation, while progestins have protective effects. Unopposed estrogen exposure increases risk.
keyPoints: 66,200 new cases of uterine corpus cancers expected in 2024
13,030 deaths predicted in 2024
Estrogen is a key factor in endometrial gland proliferation
Progestins are protective against endometrial cancer
Obese women and those with estrogen-producing tumors have higher risk
Tamoxifen increases risk due to weak estrogenic effects on uterine epithelium
Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) carriers have a 4% annual risk of endometrial cancer, ~200-fold increased relative risk
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Genetic Predisposition in Endometrioid Uterine Cancer
items.content: Women with Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) are at a significantly higher risk for developing endometrioid endometrial carcinoma due to mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes. These genetic predispositions also increase risks for other cancers.
keyPoints: Lynch syndrome involves MSH2, MLH1, PMS1, and PMS2 gene mutations
4% annual risk of endometrial cancer in middle-aged women with HNPCC
~200-fold increased relative risk compared to non-carriers
Associated with family history of colon cancer and modestly increased ovarian cancer risk
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Characteristics of Endometrial Cancer Subtypes and Their Prognosis
items.content: Endometrial cancers are predominantly adenocarcinomas, with type 1 being estrogen-linked and type 2 having less clear associations. Type 2 includes serous cancers that exhibit TP53 loss of function and behave like ovarian cancers with high systemic recurrence risk.
keyPoints: 75–80% of endometrial cancers are adenocarcinomas
Type 1: estrogen-linked; Type 2: less clear associations, including serous cancers
Serous cancers show TP53 loss and high systemic recurrence risk
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Endometrial Cancer
items.content: Most women with endometrial cancer present with postmenopausal vaginal bleeding. Premenopausal women may have atypical bleeding between cycles, often leading to early-stage diagnosis via endometrial biopsy.
keyPoints: Postmenopausal vaginal bleeding is a common presentation
Premenopausal women may experience atypical bleeding
Endometrial biopsy is used for diagnosis
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.title: Treatment and Prognosis of Endometrial Cancer by Stage
items.content: Localized endometrial cancer (75% stage I) is treated with hysterectomy. Sentinel node resection provides staging information, while high-grade or deeply invasive tumors may require intravaginal brachytherapy to prevent recurrence.
keyPoints: 75% of cases are stage I
Hysterectomy is the definitive treatment for localized disease
Intravaginal brachytherapy prevents recurrence in high-risk cases
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.title: Understanding Risk Factors for Gestational Trophoblastic Tumors
items.content: Gestational trophoblastic tumors, including molar pregnancies and choriocarcinoma, are influenced by various risk factors. These neoplasias often arise from abnormal fertilization events leading to persistent placental tissue growth. Key risk factors include age extremes in reproductive years, dietary deficiencies, and environmental influences.
keyPoints: Trophoblastic tumors result from the outgrowth or persistence of placental tissue.
Incidence is higher in females conceiving younger than 16 or older than 50; incidence can be as high as one in three in older women due to abnormal fertilization risks.
Dietary factors low in carotene and animal fats are associated with higher molar pregnancy rates, especially noted in Southeast Asia.
Most trophoblastic neoplasms (complete moles) involve paternal uniparental disomy from a single sperm fertilizing an enucleate egg.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · GESTATIONAL TROPHOBLASTIC TUMORS | RISK FACTORS

items.title: Differential Incidence and Pathogenesis of Molar Pregnancies
items.content: Molar pregnancies, a type of gestational trophoblastic tumor, have varying incidence rates globally. Complete moles are diploid with paternal genetic material only, while partial moles are triploid due to fertilization by two sperm cells. The risk for persistent disease is higher in complete moles.
keyPoints: Approximately 1 in 1500 conceptions result in molar pregnancies in developed Western countries; incidence varies globally.
Complete moles arise from paternal uniparental disomy, while partial moles are triploid due to two sperm fertilizing an egg.
Risk of persistent gestational trophoblastic disease is ~5% after a partial mole.
Myometrial invasion occurs in no more than one in six complete moles and less frequently in partial moles.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · GESTATIONAL TROPHOBLASTIC TUMORS | RISK FACTORS

items.title: Early Detection and Management of Molar Pregnancy
items.content: In developed countries, early detection of molar pregnancy via home pregnancy kits and ultrasound allows for timely intervention. Most women with complete or partial moles experience spontaneous remission post-uterine evacuation, monitored by serial serum $\beta$-hCG levels, requiring no chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Early detection leads to typical early pregnancy symptoms: nausea, amenorrhea, breast tenderness.
Most women achieve spontaneous remission after uterine evacuation of moles.
15-25% of women show persistent gestational trophoblastic disease post-evacuation.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PRESENTATION OF INVASIVE TROPHOBLASTIC DISEASE

items.title: Prognosis and Treatment of Persistent Gestational Trophoblastic Disease
items.content: Persistent or rising $\beta$-hCG post-evacuation indicates the need for therapy. Prognostic scoring algorithms help determine treatment risk levels, with high-risk patients requiring multiagent chemotherapy at expert centers.
keyPoints: Persistent elevation of $\beta$-hCG necessitates therapeutic intervention.
Prognosis determined by scoring algorithms categorizes risk as low, intermediate, or high.
High-risk factors include widely metastatic nonpulmonary disease and very elevated $\beta$-hCG levels.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PRESENTATION OF INVASIVE TROPHOBLASTIC DISEASE

items.title: Management Strategy for Persistent and Rising $ \beta $-hCG in Invasive Trophoblastic Disease
items.content: For patients with invasive trophoblastic disease who exhibit persistent and rising $ \beta $-hCG levels after molar evacuation, the primary treatment is chemotherapy. Surgery may be considered for isolated uterine disease or to control hemorrhage, particularly if childbearing has been completed. Chemotherapy resistance warrants surgical intervention.
keyPoints: Management should aim for 100% cure and be handled by experienced clinicians.
$ \beta $-hCG levels guide treatment decisions.
Chemotherapy is the primary approach for persistent and rising $ \beta $-hCG.
Surgery can manage chemotherapy-resistant disease isolated in the uterus or control hemorrhage.
Childbearing status influences surgical intervention.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Invasive Trophoblastic Disease

items.title: Treatment Approaches Based on Risk Stratification in Invasive Trophoblastic Disease
items.content: The treatment of invasive trophoblastic disease varies based on risk stratification. Low-risk patients, defined by lower $ \beta $-hCG levels and earlier presentation post-pregnancy, are often treated with single-agent chemotherapy (dactinomycin or methotrexate), curing 90% of cases. High-risk patients, identified by very high $ \beta $-hCG levels, late presentation ($ \geq $4 months after pregnancy), metastases to the brain or liver, or failure of initial methotrexate therapy, require multiagent chemotherapy (EMA-CO) for cure. The highest risk group with extensive metastases may receive a regimen including cisplatin and etoposide.
keyPoints: Low-risk disease is treated with single-agent dactinomycin or methotrexate, curing 90% of cases.
High-risk patients need multiagent chemotherapy (EMA-CO).
Highest-risk patients might require cisplatin/etoposide alternating with etoposide/methotrexate/dactinomycin.
Risk factors include high $ \beta $-hCG levels, late presentation ($ \geq $4 months), and metastases to brain or liver.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Invasive Trophoblastic Disease

items.question: Which histologic variant of ovarian neoplasms is most common?
items.explanation: Epithelial tumors are the most common histologic variant of ovarian neoplasms.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Gynecologic Malignancies | INCIDENCE AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of epithelial ovarian tumors is frankly malignant?
items.explanation: 33% of epithelial ovarian tumors are frankly malignant.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Gynecologic Malignancies | INCIDENCE AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: What is the term used for bilateral ovarian masses from metastatic mucin-secreting gastrointestinal cancers?
items.explanation: Bilateral ovarian masses from metastatic mucin-secreting gastrointestinal cancers are termed Krukenberg tumors.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Gynecologic Malignancies | INCIDENCE AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following factors is NOT linked to an increased risk of developing ovarian cancer?
items.explanation: The use of oral contraceptives is a protective factor against ovarian cancer, not a risk factor.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.question: Which genetic mutation is most commonly associated with type 2 serous epithelial ovarian cancers?
items.explanation: Type 2 serous epithelial ovarian cancers are classically characterized by the loss of TP53 function.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.question: What is a recommended prophylactic measure for women with high-risk germline BRCA1/2 mutations?
options: Prophylactic removal of fallopian tubes and ovaries
items.explanation: Women with high-risk germline BRCA1/2 mutations are advised to undergo prophylactic removal of fallopian tubes and ovaries.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.question: Which type of ovarian cancer is characterized by low-grade histology and generally indolent behavior?
items.explanation: Type 1 ovarian cancers are characterized by low-grade histology and generally indolent behavior.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is least likely to be associated with advanced ovarian cancer?
items.explanation: Advanced ovarian cancer typically presents with gastrointestinal and abdominal symptoms, not fever.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 2)

items.question: What is the most common stage at presentation for women diagnosed with ovarian cancer?
items.explanation: About 70% of women present with stage III disease.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 2)

items.question: Which laboratory marker is commonly elevated in ovarian cancer but not specific to it?
items.explanation: Serum CA-125, the shed mucin component (MUC16), is associated with but not specific for ovarian cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 2)

items.question: What is the preferred initial diagnostic and therapeutic option for an isolated adnexal mass in ovarian cancer?
options: Surgery by a skilled gynecologic oncologist
Primary chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin
items.explanation: Surgery by a skilled gynecologic oncologist is the preferred initial diagnostic and therapeutic option for an isolated adnexal mass.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.question: What is contraindicated to avoid malignant contamination of the peritoneal cavity in ovarian cancer?
items.explanation: Needle biopsy of adnexal masses is contraindicated to avoid malignant contamination of the peritoneal cavity.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.question: What is the target outcome of an ovarian cancer surgery?
options: Optimal resection with residual disease >1 cm
items.explanation: The target outcome of an ovarian cancer surgery is always an R0 resection, meaning no visible residual cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.question: What treatment strategy improves survival outcomes in women with functional homologous DNA repair defects?
items.explanation: Administration of oral PARPi such as niraparib, olaparib, or rucaparib improves survival outcomes in women with functional homologous DNA repair defects.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.question: Which of the following features are linked to a more aggressive behavior in Low Malignant Potential (Borderline) Ovarian Tumors?
items.explanation: Micropapillary histology and microinvasion are linked to more aggressive behavior in Low Malignant Potential Ovarian Tumors.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.question: What is the typical management approach for patients with tumors of low malignant potential?
items.explanation: Tumors of low malignant potential are primarily managed by surgery.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.question: Which type of ovarian tumor is most commonly associated with the production of androgens, leading to hirsutism and virilization?
items.explanation: Sertoli-Leydig tumors often present with hirsutism and virilization due to increased production of androgens.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.question: What is the most common germ cell neoplasm of the ovary?
items.explanation: Benign teratoma, or dermoid cyst, is the most common germ cell neoplasm of the ovary.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.question: Which type of histology do fallopian tube malignancies typically exhibit?
items.explanation: Fallopian tube malignancies are typically of serous histology.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.question: What is the primary neoplastic-initiating event in the majority of women with invasive cervical cancer?
items.explanation: Infection with high-risk strains of HPV is the primary neoplastic-initiating event in the majority of women with invasive cervical cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.question: Which HPV types are most frequently associated with high-grade dysplasia?
items.explanation: HPV16 and 18 are the types most frequently associated with high-grade dysplasia.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.question: What is a characteristic mutagenesis pattern found in cervical tumors according to The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) study?
items.explanation: Cervical tumors showed a characteristic APOBEC pattern of mutagenesis.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.question: Which method is primarily used to detect asymptomatic preinvasive cervical dysplasia during a gynecologic exam?
items.explanation: The Pap smear is the primary detection method for asymptomatic preinvasive cervical dysplasia.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · HPV INFECTION AND PREVENTION

items.question: What is a limitation of incorporating HPV testing by PCR in cervical pathology screening?
options: It identifies many women with transient infections who require no specific medical intervention.
It requires more frequent screenings than Pap smears.
It eliminates the need for Pap smear screening.
items.explanation: HPV testing by PCR increases sensitivity but identifies many women with transient infections who require no specific medical intervention.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · HPV INFECTION AND PREVENTION

items.question: What is a primary prevention strategy for HPV infection?
items.explanation: A primary prevention strategy relies on HPV vaccines.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · HPV INFECTION AND PREVENTION

items.question: Which factor is NOT associated with an increased risk of cervical dysplasia?
options: Low CD4+ T-cell counts in HIV infection
items.explanation: Low CD4+ T-cell counts are associated with a higher rate of high-grade dysplasia, not specifically cervical dysplasia.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.question: What is the most common histological type of cervical malignancy?
items.explanation: The majority (80%) of cervical malignancies are squamous cell carcinomas.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.question: Which symptom suggests advanced-stage cervical cancer?
items.explanation: Pelvic or sacral pain indicates lateral extension into the pelvic nerve plexus, suggesting advanced-stage disease.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.question: Which imaging modality is more accurate for estimating uterine and paracervical extension of cervical cancer?
items.explanation: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is more accurate at estimating uterine and paracervical extension of disease.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.question: Which histologic subtype is the most common gynecologic malignancy in the United States?
items.explanation: The endometrioid histologic subtype is the most common gynecologic malignancy in the United States.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is a principal risk factor for the development of endometrioid tumors?
items.explanation: Relative overexposure to estrogens is the principal risk factor for the development of endometrioid tumors.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which genetic condition significantly increases the risk for endometrioid endometrial carcinoma?
items.explanation: Women with Lynch syndrome, also known as HNPCC, are at increased risk for endometrioid endometrial carcinoma.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which type of endometrial cancer is associated with TP53 loss of function and has a clinical behavior similar to ovarian cancers?
items.explanation: Endometrial serous cancers show TP53 loss of function and behave clinically more like ovarian cancers.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.question: What is the typical presenting symptom for women with tumors of the uterine corpus?
items.explanation: The majority of women with tumors of the uterine corpus present with postmenopausal vaginal bleeding.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.question: What is the definitive treatment for most women with localized endometrial cancer?
options: Hysterectomy with removal of ovaries and fallopian tubes
items.explanation: Definitive treatment typically involves a hysterectomy with removal of the ovaries and fallopian tubes.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.question: Which therapy is part of the standard care for advanced-stage endometrial cancers?
options: Carboplatin and paclitaxel combinations with immune targeting agents
Adjuvant chemotherapy in stage I–II disease
items.explanation: Carboplatin and paclitaxel combinations with immune targeting agents are the current standard of care for advanced-stage endometrial cancers.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.question: Which dietary factor is associated with higher incidence rates of molar pregnancy?
options: High carotene and animal fats diet
Low carotene and animal fats diet
items.explanation: Regions with high molar pregnancy rates often have diets low in carotene and animal fats.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · GESTATIONAL TROPHOBLASTIC TUMORS | RISK FACTORS

items.question: At what age is the incidence of molar pregnancy particularly high due to increased risk of abnormal fertilization?
items.explanation: The incidence is particularly high in females conceiving under age 16 or older than age 50.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · GESTATIONAL TROPHOBLASTIC TUMORS | RISK FACTORS

items.question: What genetic condition characterizes complete moles?
options: Diploid tumors with all maternal genetic material
Triploid tumors with two-thirds paternal genetic material
Diploid tumors with all paternal genetic material (uniparental disomy)
Haploid tumors with equal maternal and paternal genetic material
items.explanation: Complete moles are diploid tumors with all genetic material from the paternal donor.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · GESTATIONAL TROPHOBLASTIC TUMORS | RISK FACTORS

items.question: Which of the following symptoms are typically seen in women with early detection of molar pregnancy?
items.explanation: Early detection of molar pregnancy typically presents with symptoms of early pregnancy such as nausea, amenorrhea, and breast tenderness.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PRESENTATION OF INVASIVE TROPHOBLASTIC DISEASE

items.question: What is the typical management for women who experience spontaneous remission after uterine evacuation of early complete and partial moles?
options: Multiagent chemotherapy at an expert center
items.explanation: Most women experience spontaneous remission after uterine evacuation and require no chemotherapy.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PRESENTATION OF INVASIVE TROPHOBLASTIC DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following is a potential life-threatening complication associated with the evacuation of large moles?
items.explanation: Evacuation of large moles can be associated with life-threatening complications such as uterine perforation.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PRESENTATION OF INVASIVE TROPHOBLASTIC DISEASE

items.question: What is the typical initial treatment for persistent and rising $ \beta $-hCG after evacuation of a molar conception?
items.explanation: The typical initial treatment for persistent and rising $ \beta $-hCG after evacuation of a molar conception is chemotherapy.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Invasive Trophoblastic Disease

items.question: Which single-agent treatments are used to cure 90% of women with low-risk invasive trophoblastic disease?
items.explanation: Single-agent treatment with dactinomycin or methotrexate cures 90% of women with low-risk disease.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Invasive Trophoblastic Disease

items.question: What is the preferred treatment for women with metastatic invasive trophoblastic disease who wish to maintain fertility?
options: Chemotherapy or immunotherapy targeting the PD-1 axis
items.explanation: For women wishing to maintain fertility with metastatic disease, the preferred treatment is chemotherapy or immunotherapy targeting the PD-1 axis.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Invasive Trophoblastic Disease

items.statement: Most stromal tumors produce estrogen.
items.explanation: Most stromal tumors do not produce estrogen, though certain subtypes can.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Gynecologic Malignancies | INCIDENCE AND PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Age influences the risk of malignancy in adnexal masses.
items.explanation: In younger women, tumors are more likely to be benign.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Gynecologic Malignancies | INCIDENCE AND PATHOLOGY

items.statement: The majority of ovarian cancer cases in the United States are expected to be diagnosed as type 2 serous epithelial cancers.
items.explanation: Type 2 serous epithelial ovarian cancers are more aggressive and common.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.statement: BRCA1/2 mutations do not increase the risk of breast cancer.
items.explanation: Individuals with BRCA1/2 mutations have an increased lifetime risk of developing breast cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.statement: PET scans are generally required in the evaluation of advanced ovarian cancer.
items.explanation: Positron emission tomography (PET) scans are generally not required for evaluating advanced ovarian cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 2)

items.statement: Screening for ovarian cancer is currently recommended outside of clinical trials.
items.explanation: Screening for ovarian cancer is not recommended outside of a clinical trial.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 2)

items.statement: Full lymphadenectomy provides significant therapeutic advantage over nodal sampling in ovarian cancer.
items.explanation: Full lymphadenectomy appears to provide little or no additional therapeutic advantage over nodal sampling.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.statement: Patients with R0 resection after surgery have a median survival of less than 60 months.
items.explanation: Patients without gross residual disease (R0 resection) after resection have a median survival in excess of 60 months.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.statement: Stromal tumors are typically associated with high metastatic potential.
items.explanation: Most stromal tumors have limited metastatic potential and present as unilateral solid masses.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.statement: Bilateral ovarian germ cell tumors are common in dysgerminoma.
items.explanation: Dysgerminomas can be bilateral, occurring in 10–15% of cases.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.statement: Fallopian tube cancers have a natural history and treatment that are essentially identical to ovarian cancer.
items.explanation: The passage states that fallopian tubal cancers have a natural history and treatment that are essentially identical to ovarian cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.statement: HPV-negative cervical cancers are more common in younger women.
items.explanation: HPV-negative cervical cancers are more common in older women.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.statement: Vaccination of girls and women between ages 9 and 45 years is recommended with three injections at intervals of 0, 2, and 6 months.
items.explanation: The Gardasil-9 vaccine schedule includes three injections at 0, 2, and 6 months.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · HPV INFECTION AND PREVENTION

items.statement: Vaccinated women no longer need standard Pap smear screening.
items.explanation: Even vaccinated women are still at risk for HPV infection and benefit from standard Pap smear screening.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · HPV INFECTION AND PREVENTION

items.statement: Smoking increases the risk of dysplasia in individuals with HPV infection.
items.explanation: Smoking is a cofactor that increases the risk of dysplasia with HPV infection.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.statement: CT scans are more accurate than MRI for evaluating pelvic structures in cervical cancer.
items.explanation: MRI is more accurate at estimating uterine extension and paracervical extension of disease.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.statement: Tamoxifen treatment is associated with a decreased risk of endometrial cancer.
items.explanation: Long-term treatment with tamoxifen is associated with an increased risk of endometrial cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Obese women have a higher risk for developing endometrial cancer.
items.explanation: Obese women are at higher risk for endometrial cancer due to increased estrogen exposure.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Resection of lymph nodes improves outcome in endometrial cancer treatment.
items.explanation: The resection of lymph nodes does not improve outcome, but sentinel node resection provides important staging and prognostic information.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.statement: Women with high-grade tumors or deeply invasive tumors are at higher risk for pelvic recurrence.
items.explanation: Patients with high-grade tumors or tumors that are deeply invasive (stage IB) are at higher risk for pelvic recurrence.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.statement: Most trophoblastic neoplasms arise in the fallopian tubes.
items.explanation: Trophoblastic tumors most commonly arise in the uterus.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · GESTATIONAL TROPHOBLASTIC TUMORS | RISK FACTORS

items.statement: The risk of persistent gestational trophoblastic disease after a partial mole is approximately 5%.
items.explanation: The passage states that the risk of persistent disease after a partial mole is ~5%.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · GESTATIONAL TROPHOBLASTIC TUMORS | RISK FACTORS

items.statement: Most women with persistent elevation or rising $ \beta $-hCG after molar evacuation require chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Women with persistent or actively growing gestational trophoblastic disease indicated by elevated or rising $ \beta $-hCG levels typically need therapy.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PRESENTATION OF INVASIVE TROPHOBLASTIC DISEASE

items.statement: Even very advanced gestational trophoblastic disease is not curable when managed by an expert.
items.explanation: Very advanced gestational trophoblastic disease is almost uniformly curable with management by an expert.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PRESENTATION OF INVASIVE TROPHOBLASTIC DISEASE

items.statement: Trophoblastic disease is highly sensitive to chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Trophoblastic disease is exquisitely sensitive to chemotherapy.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Invasive Trophoblastic Disease

items.statement: Cured women with invasive trophoblastic disease cannot become pregnant without increased fetal or maternal complications.
items.explanation: Cured women may become pregnant again without evidence of increased fetal or maternal complications.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Invasive Trophoblastic Disease

items.assertion: Epithelial ovarian tumors have multiple histologic variants.
items.reason: Each variant has unique neoplastic behaviors linked to different cell populations in the ovary.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Gynecologic Malignancies | INCIDENCE AND PATHOLOGY

items.assertion: Prophylactic salpingectomy is a key part of surgical prophylaxis for ovarian cancer prevention.
items.reason: Salpingo-oophorectomy reduces the risk of subsequent breast cancer by 50%.
items.explanation: While both statements are true, the reason does not directly explain why salpingectomy is key; it's about preventing ovarian cancer.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.assertion: Type 2 serous epithelial ovarian cancers are characterized by mutations in KRAS and BRAF.
items.reason: These genetic alterations lead to intraepithelial cancer in the luminal epithelium of the fallopian tube.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; type 2 serous cancers are characterized by TP53 mutations, not KRAS and BRAF.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · OVARIAN CANCER OF EPITHELIAL ORIGIN (part 1)

items.assertion: Needle biopsy is contraindicated for adnexal masses due to risk of malignant contamination.
items.reason: Malignant cells can contaminate the peritoneal cavity during needle biopsy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why needle biopsy is contraindicated.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.assertion: Intraperitoneal chemotherapy offers superior results compared to intravenous administration.
items.reason: The intraperitoneal approach is technically more difficult and increasingly replaced by carboplatin and paclitaxel.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; intraperitoneal chemotherapy does not offer superior results. The reason is true but unrelated to the assertion.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · Ovarian Cancer

items.assertion: Germ cell tumors have a higher proclivity for nodal or hematogenous metastases compared to epithelial ovarian cancer.
items.reason: These tumors typically present in women of childbearing age and often become large ovarian masses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the higher proclivity for metastases.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.assertion: The treatment of stromal tumors typically involves adjuvant chemotherapy.
items.reason: Chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel is generally reserved for unresectable or multiply recurrent tumors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the primary treatment for stromal tumors is complete surgical resection, not adjuvant chemotherapy.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · UNCOMMON OVARIAN TUMORS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.assertion: The majority of type 2 ovarian cancers arise from the tubal epithelium.
items.reason: Type 2 ovarian cancers are now thought to originate in the fallopian tube rather than the ovary itself.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS
items.assertion: HPV16 and HPV18 are low-risk types associated with cervical cancer.
items.reason: These HPV types are known to generate high-grade dysplasia and malignancy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because HPV16 and HPV18 are high-risk types, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · FALLOPIAN TUBE CANCER | ETIOLOGY AND GENETICS

items.assertion: Vaccination before the initiation of sexual activity reduces the rate of high-risk HPV infection.
items.reason: The vaccine is effective in preventing infections with specific high-risk HPV types.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why vaccination reduces high-risk HPV infection.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · HPV INFECTION AND PREVENTION

items.assertion: Minimally invasive surgery has an inferior outcome compared to standard open hysterectomy for very small stage I cervical tumors.
items.reason: Standard open hysterectomy provides better outcomes than minimally invasive surgery for these tumors.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason are both true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.assertion: Radiographic studies are part of the formal clinical staging of cervical cancer.
items.reason: Treatment planning requires radiographic studies for appropriate therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; radiographic studies are not part of formal clinical staging, but they are required for treatment planning.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · CLINICAL PRESENTATIONS

items.assertion: Endometrial cancer prognosis depends on stage, histologic grade, and depth of myometrial invasion.
items.reason: These factors determine the extent of disease spread and potential for recurrence.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these factors affect prognosis.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.assertion: Leiomyosarcomas of the uterus benefit from adjuvant therapy.
items.reason: These tumors are treated with docetaxel/gemcitabine at recurrence but do not appear to benefit from adjuvant therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because leiomyosarcomas of the uterus do not benefit from adjuvant therapy.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PATHOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | Uterine Cancer

items.assertion: Women presenting without access to prenatal care may experience life-threatening symptoms.
items.reason: Lack of prenatal care can lead to the development of preeclampsia or eclampsia in women with trophoblastic disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why lack of prenatal care can result in life-threatening symptoms.
items.reference: Gynecologic Malignancies · PRESENTATION OF INVASIVE TROPHOBLASTIC DISEASE
